title: Utility-scale quantum experiments using dynamic circuits to address collective dissipation in interacting qubits
authors: Benjamin Tirado, Joana Fraxanet, Adrián Juan-Delgado, Javier Aizpurua, Ruben Esteban
abstract: Open quantum systems are central to quantum optics, condensed matter, and chemistry, yet their simulation remains challenging for both classical and near-term quantum hardware. In this work we implement and execute utility-scale quantum circuits that accurately reproduce the dissipative dynamics of interacting qubits. We consider a one-dimensional chain of many qubits weakly coupled to a common Markovian bath. The Markovian time evolution of the system is implemented through Trotterized evolution with the introduction of ancilla-assisted dissipative channels, including single-qubit and two-qubit dissipators to capture collective decay. Mid-circuit measurements, conditional gates, and hardware-aware transpilation significantly reduce circuit depth. We further implement a biased Clifford data regression (biased CDR), an error mitigation strategy that outperforms the uniform Cliffordization baseline and a variety of zero-noise extrapolation protocols. We execute large-scale quantum experiments of the dynamics of chains comprising up to 86 emitters on the IBM System Two \texttt{ibm\_basquecountry}. In order to do so, we use 129 total qubits (including ancillas), with the largest circuits contain about 8000 two-qubit gates. To validate these experiments we develop a classical Monte Carlo-Time-Evolving Block-Decimation (MC-TEBD) tensor-network method that incorporates reset operations through stochastic pure-state trajectories, obtaining very good agreement. The approach presented here opens a practical route for utility-scale quantum simulation of dissipative dynamics, enabled by dynamic circuits, targeted error mitigation, and tensor-network validation, and enables to tackle complex dynamics of systems such as quantum emitters in dissipative optical cavities.
summary: The paper demonstrates utility-scale quantum simulations of collective dissipation in large interacting qubit chains using IBM's System Two processor. By combining dynamic circuits, hardware-aware transpilation, and advanced error mitigation like biased CDR, the authors successfully model complex dissipative dynamics in systems of up to 86 emitters. This approach provides a scalable route for studying many-body open quantum systems on near-term quantum hardware.
main_problem: Simulating the complex dissipative dynamics of large-scale interacting qubit chains on near-term quantum hardware, specifically addressing the challenges of collective decay and high circuit depth.
main_result: The authors successfully executed utility-scale experiments on the IBM System Two processor using up to 129 qubits, demonstrating that hardware-aware dynamic circuits and biased Clifford Data Regression (CDR) can accurately reproduce collective dissipative phenomena.
method: The approach uses Trotterized evolution with ancilla-assisted dissipative channels, optimized via mid-circuit measurements, conditional gates, and hardware-aware transpilation. Error mitigation is achieved through dynamical decoupling and biased CDR, while classical validation is performed using a hybrid Monte Carlo-TEBD tensor-network method.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional chain of interacting two-level quantum emitters coupled to a common Markovian bath, described by a Lindblad master equation featuring single-qupi decay and collective (cross-qubit) dissipation.
key_observables: Excited-state populations, density matrix elements (rho_00, rho_11, rho_01), and coherences.
important_parameters: Scales up to 86 emitters and 129 total qubits; largest circuits contain ~8,000 two-qubit gates; effective parameters include frequency (1.2045 eV), coupling (4.5 meV), and decay rates (9 meV).
main_assumptions: The system is treated within the Markovian approximation and the weak-coupling limit, neglecting long-range interactions beyond nearest-neighbor coupling.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows system-bath coupling and circuit architecture; Figure 2 demonstrates single-qubit decay validation; Figure 3 illustrates the interaction basis and two-qubit dynamics; Figure 4 compares circuit depth scaling for different transpilation strategies; Figure 5 evaluates error mitigation performance (ZNE and CDR); Figure 11 characterizes the IBM Heron r2 QPU metrics.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of simulating open quantum systems, moves to the construction of the Trotterized circuit and basis transformation for collective decay, details the hardware-aware optimizations and error mitigation strategies, presents large-scale experimental results on IBM hardware, and concludes with classical validation using a hybrid tensor-network approach.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it provides a practical, scalable blueprint for simulating many-body open quantum systems (like superradiance) on NISQ hardware, bridging the gap between theoretical Lindblad dynamics and large-scale experimental execution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Dissipative phenomena in systems of qubits coupled to a common external bath and modeling of the dynamics using quantum circuits. (a) Schematic of the coupling between the system of interest (S) and the external bath (B). The system of interest consists of n qubits, depicted as purple circles and modeled as two-level systems with transition frequencies ωi and spontaneous decay rates γi (i = 0, 1, ..., n −1). Each of the qubits is coupled with strength giB (i = 0, 1, ..., n −1) to a common external bath, depicted in yellow, which has a single mode of frequency ωB and a dissipation rate κB. (b) Schematic of the Trotterized quantum circuit modelling Markovian time evolution under...

FIG. 2. Modeling of the dissipative dynamics of a qubit. (a) Schematic of the effective removal of the cavity through the Markovian approximation. The quantum emitter (purple sphere) is considered to be a two-level system with transition frequency ω0 and spontaneous decay rate γ0 and the cav- ity (blue sphere) has a single mode of frequency ωA and a dissipation rate of κA. Both the quantum emitter and the cavity are originally coupled with coupling strength g0B. Af- ter removing the cavity, the properties of the emitter (now pictured with a color gradient) are modified, resulting in a new effective frequency ˜ω0 and effective decay rate ˜γ0. (b) Time-parametric quantum circuit representing...

FIG. 3. Modeling of the collective dissipative dynamics of two qubits (representing two quantum emitters) coupled through a common environment (an optical cavity). (a) Schematic of the system after performing the effective removal of the cavity through the Markovian approximation, resulting in two inter- acting two-level systems labeled q0 and q1 and pictured as color-graded spheres. The Markovian approximation results in the modification of the properties of the quantum emitters (with new transition frequency ˜ω and decay rate ˜γ), and also in the emergence of a new effective coupling ˜g0,1 and a cross- dissipation term ˜γ0,1. (b) Energy level schemes of the com- putational (left) and...

FIG. 4. Near-term hardware-aware transformation of the circuit in Fig. 3c. (a) Scaling with the number of emitters n of the two-qubit depth of the virtual (dashed lines) and ISA circuits obtained after transpilation (solid lines) for the original circuit in Fig. 3c (yellow triangles), the dynamic circuit in Fig. 4b (purple squares) and the hardware-aware dynamic circuit in Fig. 4c (blue circles). (b) Dynamic circuit that models the collective dissipation of two emitters. This circuit is the first adaptation of the original circuit in Fig. 3c into a dynamical circuit, which achieves a significant two-qubit depth reduction. FIG. 5. Comparison of the performance of error suppres- sion and mitigation techniques in ibm basquecountry using the hardware-aware circuit in Fig. 4c extended to a chain of ten quantum emitters. (a) Colormap of the absolute error of the excited-state population of each system qubit qi, with i = 0, ..., 9 at ˜γt = 0.45.
